question:
7. If \( u_{1}{ }^{2}+u_{2}{ }^{2}+\cdots \) and \( v_{1}{ }^{2}+v_{2}{ }^{2}+\cdots \) are convergent series of real constants, prove that
\[
\left(u_{1}-v_{1}\right)^{p}+\left(u_{2}-v_{2}\right)^{p}+\cdots, p \text { an integer } \geq 2
\]
is convergent.
solution:
Solution. Let \( A=u_{1}{ }^{2}+u_{2}{ }^{2}+\cdots \) and \( B=v_{1}{ }^{2}+v_{2}{ }^{2}+\cdots \). Since
\[
\left(u_{i}+v_{i}\right)^{2}+\left(u_{i}-v_{i}\right)^{2}=2 u_{i}^{2}+2 v_{i}^{2}
\]
we have, for any positive integer \( n \),
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(u_{i}-v_{i}\right)^{2} \leq 2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} u_{i}^{2}+2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_{i}^{2} \leq 2 A+2 B
\]

Since the terms are all non-negative, it follows that
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{\infty}\left(u_{i}-v_{i}\right)^{2} \text { is convergent. }
\]

Therefore, the terms approach zero, so there exists an integer \( \boldsymbol{k} \) such that
\[
\left(u_{i}-v_{i}\right)^{2}<1 \quad \text { for all } i \geq k
\]

If \( p \) is an integer and \( p \geq 2 \), then \( \left|u_{i}-v_{i}\right|^{p} \leq\left(u_{i}-v_{i}\right)^{2} \) for all \( i \geq k \), so the series
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{\infty}\left(u_{i}-v_{i}\right)^{p}
\]
is absolutely convergent, and therefore convergent.

descriptive_long.question:
7. If \( firstuvalue^{2}+seconduvalue^{2}+\cdots \) and \( firstvvalue^{2}+secondvvalue^{2}+\cdots \) are convergent series of real constants, prove that
\[
\left(firstuvalue-firstvvalue\right)^{powerparam}+\left(seconduvalue-secondvvalue\right)^{powerparam}+\cdots,\; powerparam \text { an integer } \geq 2
\]
is convergent.
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Let \( sumuconst=firstuvalue^{2}+seconduvalue^{2}+\cdots \) and \( sumvconst=firstvvalue^{2}+secondvvalue^{2}+\cdots \). Since
\[
\left(generaluvalue+generalvvalue\right)^{2}+\left(generaluvalue-generalvvalue\right)^{2}=2\,generaluvalue^{2}+2\,generalvvalue^{2}
\]
we have, for any positive integer \( termcount \),
\[
\sum_{indexvar=1}^{termcount}\left(generaluvalue-generalvvalue\right)^{2}\le 2\sum_{indexvar=1}^{termcount}generaluvalue^{2}+2\sum_{indexvar=1}^{termcount}generalvvalue^{2}\le 2\,sumuconst+2\,sumvconst
\]

Since the terms are all non-negative, it follows that
\[
\sum_{indexvar=1}^{\infty}\left(generaluvalue-generalvvalue\right)^{2}\text{ is convergent.}
\]

Therefore, the terms approach zero, so there exists an integer \( \boldsymbol{cutoffidx} \) such that
\[
\left(generaluvalue-generalvvalue\right)^{2}<1\quad \text{for all } indexvar \ge cutoffidx
\]

If \( powerparam \) is an integer and \( powerparam \ge 2 \), then \( \lvert generaluvalue-generalvvalue \rvert^{powerparam}\le\left(generaluvalue-generalvvalue\right)^{2} \) for all \( indexvar \ge cutoffidx \), so the series
\[
\sum_{indexvar=1}^{\infty}\left(generaluvalue-generalvvalue\right)^{powerparam}
\]
is absolutely convergent, and therefore convergent.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
7. If \( sunflower^{2}+paintbrush^{2}+\cdots \) and \( tangerine^{2}+jellyfish^{2}+\cdots \) are convergent series of real constants, prove that
\[
\left(sunflower-tangerine\right)^{dragonfly}+\left(paintbrush-jellyfish\right)^{dragonfly}+\cdots, dragonfly \text { an integer } \geq 2
\]
is convergent.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Let \( labyrinth=sunflower^{2}+paintbrush^{2}+\cdots \) and \( butterfly=tangerine^{2}+jellyfish^{2}+\cdots \). Since
\[
\left(chandelier+sailboat\right)^{2}+\left(chandelier-sailboat\right)^{2}=2 chandelier^{2}+2 sailboat^{2}
\]
we have, for any positive integer \( earthquake \),
\[
\sum_{waterfall=1}^{earthquake}\left(chandelier-sailboat\right)^{2} \leq 2 \sum_{waterfall=1}^{earthquake} chandelier^{2}+2 \sum_{waterfall=1}^{earthquake} sailboat^{2} \leq 2 labyrinth+2 butterfly
\]

Since the terms are all non-negative, it follows that
\[
\sum_{waterfall=1}^{\infty}\left(chandelier-sailboat\right)^{2} \text { is convergent. }
\]

Therefore, the terms approach zero, so there exists an integer \( \boldsymbol{lighthouse} \) such that
\[
\left(chandelier-sailboat\right)^{2}<1 \quad \text { for all } waterfall \geq lighthouse
\]

If \( dragonfly \) is an integer and \( dragonfly \geq 2 \), then \( \left|chandelier-sailboat\right|^{dragonfly} \leq\left(chandelier-sailboat\right)^{2} \) for all \( waterfall \geq lighthouse \), so the series
\[
\sum_{waterfall=1}^{\infty}\left(chandelier-sailboat\right)^{dragonfly}
\]
is absolutely convergent, and therefore convergent.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
7. If \( downfirst^{2}+downsecond^{2}+\cdots \) and \( scalarfirst^{2}+scalarsecond^{2}+\cdots \) are convergent series of real constants, prove that
\[
\left(downfirst-scalarfirst\right)^{weakness}+\left(downsecond-scalarsecond\right)^{weakness}+\cdots, weakness \text { an integer } \geq 2
\]
is convergent.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. Let \( emptiness=downfirst^{2}+downsecond^{2}+\cdots \) and \( fullness=scalarfirst^{2}+scalarsecond^{2}+\cdots \). Since
\[
\left(downindex+scalarindex\right)^{2}+\left(downindex-scalarindex\right)^{2}=2 downindex^{2}+2 scalarindex^{2}
\]
we have, for any positive integer \( unnatural \),
\[
\sum_{contentvalue=1}^{unnatural}\left(downindex-scalarindex\right)^{2} \leq 2 \sum_{contentvalue=1}^{unnatural} downindex^{2}+2 \sum_{contentvalue=1}^{unnatural} scalarindex^{2} \leq 2 emptiness+2 fullness
\]

Since the terms are all non-negative, it follows that
\[
\sum_{contentvalue=1}^{\infty}\left(downindex-scalarindex\right)^{2} \text { is convergent. }
\]

Therefore, the terms approach zero, so there exists an integer \( \boldsymbol{finality} \) such that
\[
\left(downindex-scalarindex\right)^{2}<1 \quad \text { for all } contentvalue \geq finality
\]

If \( weakness \) is an integer and \( weakness \geq 2 \), then \( \left|downindex-scalarindex\right|^{weakness} \leq\left(downindex-scalarindex\right)^{2} \) for all \( contentvalue \geq finality \), so the series
\[
\sum_{contentvalue=1}^{\infty}\left(downindex-scalarindex\right)^{weakness}
\]
is absolutely convergent, and therefore convergent.

garbled_string.question:
7. If \( qzxwvtnp^{2}+hjgrksla^{2}+\cdots \) and \( lkjhgfds^{2}+poiuytre^{2}+\cdots \) are convergent series of real constants, prove that
\[
\left(qzxwvtnp-lkjhgfds\right)^{edcrfvtg}+\left(hjgrksla-poiuytre\right)^{edcrfvtg}+\cdots, edcrfvtg \text { an integer } \geq 2
\]
is convergent.
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Let \( qwertyui=qzxwvtnp^{2}+hjgrksla^{2}+\cdots \) and \( qazwsxed=lkjhgfds^{2}+poiuytre^{2}+\cdots \). Since
\[
\left(mnbvcxqe+zxcvbnma\right)^{2}+\left(mnbvcxqe-zxcvbnma\right)^{2}=2 mnbvcxqe^{2}+2 zxcvbnma^{2}
\]
we have, for any positive integer \( asdfghjk \),
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{asdfghjk}\left(mnbvcxqe-zxcvbnma\right)^{2} \leq 2 \sum_{i=1}^{asdfghjk} mnbvcxqe^{2}+2 \sum_{i=1}^{asdfghjk} zxcvbnma^{2} \leq 2 qwertyui+2 qazwsxed
\]

Since the terms are all non-negative, it follows that
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{\infty}\left(mnbvcxqe-zxcvbnma\right)^{2} \text { is convergent. }
\]

Therefore, the terms approach zero, so there exists an integer \( \boldsymbol{plmoknij} \) such that
\[
\left(mnbvcxqe-zxcvbnma\right)^{2}<1 \quad \text { for all } i \geq plmoknij
\]

If \( edcrfvtg \) is an integer and \( edcrfvtg \geq 2 \), then \( \left|mnbvcxqe-zxcvbnma\right|^{edcrfvtg} \leq\left(mnbvcxqe-zxcvbnma\right)^{2} \) for all \( i \geq plmoknij \), so the series
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{\infty}\left(mnbvcxqe-zxcvbnma\right)^{edcrfvtg}
\]
is absolutely convergent, and therefore convergent.

kernel_variant.question:
Let 2 \leq  r < s < \infty  be fixed real numbers.  
For every pair (m,n)\in \mathbb{N}\times \mathbb{N} let u_{m,n}, v_{m,n}\in \mathbb{C} satisfy  

  \sum _{m=1}^{\infty }\sum _{n=1}^{\infty }|u_{m,n}|^{2}<\infty  and \sum _{m=1}^{\infty }\sum _{n=1}^{\infty }|v_{m,n}|^{2}<\infty .  

Put  

  S_{r,s}(u,v):=\sum _{m=1}^{\infty }\Bigl(\sum _{n=1}^{\infty }|u_{m,n}-v_{m,n}|^{r}\Bigr)^{s/r}.  

(a)  Prove that S_{r,s}(u,v) is finite.  

(b)  Show the quantitative estimate  

  S_{r,s}(u,v) \leq  2^{\,s/2}\Bigl( \sum _{m=1}^{\infty }\sum _{n=1}^{\infty }\bigl(|u_{m,n}|^{2}+|v_{m,n}|^{2}\bigr)\Bigr)^{s/2}.     (\star )

(c)  Prove that the constant 2^{s/2} in (\star ) is optimal: for every \varepsilon >0 there exist double-arrays (u_{m,n}), (v_{m,n}) that satisfy the square-summability hypotheses and for which  

  S_{r,s}(u,v) > (2^{s/2}-\varepsilon )\Bigl( \sum _{m,n}\bigl(|u_{m,n}|^{2}+|v_{m,n}|^{2}\bigr)\Bigr)^{s/2}.  

In particular, no smaller universal constant (depending only on r and s) can replace 2^{s/2} in (\star ).
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout set d_{m,n}:=u_{m,n}-v_{m,n}.  

Step 1.  A uniform \ell ^2-bound for the differences  
For every complex numbers u,v we have  

  |u|^{2}+|v|^{2}=\frac{1}{2}\,(|u+v|^{2}+|u-v|^{2}) \geq  \frac{1}{2}\,|u-v|^{2},  

hence  

  |d_{m,n}|^{2} \leq  2\bigl(|u_{m,n}|^{2}+|v_{m,n}|^{2}\bigr).                                              (1)

Because the right-hand side is absolutely summable,  

  D:=\sum _{m,n}|d_{m,n}|^{2} <\infty .                                                                            (2)

Step 2.  Row sums  
Define S_{m}:=\sum _{n=1}^{\infty }|d_{m,n}|^{2}.  
Then \sum _{m=1}^{\infty }S_{m}=D.                                                                                  (3)

Step 3.  Comparing row-wise \ell ^{r} and \ell ^{2} norms  
Because r \geq  2, the \ell ^{p} norms decrease with p, thus  

  (\sum _{n}|d_{m,n}|^{r})^{1/r} \leq  (\sum _{n}|d_{m,n}|^{2})^{1/2}=S_{m}^{1/2}.                                (4)

Raise (4) to the power s to obtain  

  (\sum _{n}|d_{m,n}|^{r})^{s/r} \leq  S_{m}^{s/2}.                                                             (5)

Step 4.  Splitting the index set  
Partition \mathbb{N} into  

  A := {m : S_{m} \geq  1},  B := {m : 0 \leq  S_{m}<1}.  

Because each m\in A contributes at least 1 to the sum in (3), A is finite. Therefore  

  \sum _{m\in A}S_{m}^{s/2}<\infty                                                                                     (6)

(as s/2>1).  

For m\in B we have S_{m}<1, hence S_{m}^{s/2} \leq  S_{m}.  Using (3) we get  

  \sum _{m\in B}S_{m}^{s/2} \leq  \sum _{m\in B}S_{m} \leq  D<\infty .                                                              (7)

Step 5.  Convergence of S_{r,s}(u,v)  
Combine (5), (6) and (7):  

  S_{r,s}(u,v)=\sum _{m}(\sum _{n}|d_{m,n}|^{r})^{s/r} \leq  \sum _{m}S_{m}^{s/2}<\infty .                                  (8)

This settles part (a).

Step 6.  Quantitative estimate  
Write T_{m}:=\sum _{n}\bigl(|u_{m,n}|^{2}+|v_{m,n}|^{2}\bigr).  
By (1) we have S_{m} \leq  2T_{m}.  Hence from (5)  

  (\sum _{n}|d_{m,n}|^{r})^{s/r} \leq  S_{m}^{s/2} \leq  (2T_{m})^{s/2}=2^{s/2}T_{m}^{s/2}.                        (9)

Summing over m and using the convexity inequality  
  \sum _{m}T_{m}^{s/2} \leq  (\sum _{m}T_{m})^{s/2} (s/2 > 1),  
we obtain  

  S_{r,s}(u,v) \leq  2^{s/2}\sum _{m}T_{m}^{s/2} \leq  2^{s/2}\Bigl(\sum _{m}T_{m}\Bigr)^{s/2}.                       (10)

But \sum _{m}T_{m}=\sum _{m,n}\bigl(|u_{m,n}|^{2}+|v_{m,n}|^{2}\bigr), so (10) is exactly (\star ).  
This completes part (b).

Step 7.  Optimality of the constant  
Fix \varepsilon >0 and choose N large enough so that (1+\varepsilon )^{1/s}<1+\varepsilon /2.  
Define arrays  

  u_{1,1}=1, v_{1,1}=-1, and u_{m,n}=v_{m,n}=0 for all (m,n)\neq (1,1).  

Then \sum _{m,n}(|u_{m,n}|^{2}+|v_{m,n}|^{2})=2, while  

  S_{r,s}(u,v)=|u_{1,1}-v_{1,1}|^{s}=2^{s}.  

Consequently  

  S_{r,s}(u,v)=2^{s}=2^{s/2}(2)^{s/2}  
       > (2^{s/2}-\varepsilon )(2)^{s/2} = (2^{s/2}-\varepsilon )\Bigl(\sum _{m,n}(|u_{m,n}|^{2}+|v_{m,n}|^{2})\Bigr)^{s/2}.  

Since \varepsilon >0 is arbitrary, the factor 2^{s/2} cannot be replaced by a smaller universal constant.  
This proves part (c) and finishes the solution.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher-Dimensional Structure  
   â€˘ The original task deals with a single sequence; the enhanced variant involves a double-indexed array and a mixed (â„“^{r},â„“^{s}) norm, introducing an extra layer of summation and an interaction between two different exponents.

2. Additional Interacting Exponents  
   â€˘ Two distinct exponents (r and s, with r<s) must be handled simultaneously.  The solver must understand how â„“^{p} norms behave when p varies and how to move from an inner r-norm to an outer s-norm.

3. Sophisticated Techniques  
   â€˘ The proof requires monotonicity of â„“^{p} norms, a careful rowâ€“wise decomposition, and a split into â€ślargeâ€™â€™ and â€śsmallâ€™â€™ rows to control powers exceeding 1.  
   â€˘ A quantitative estimate is demanded, forcing the solver to track constants meticulously instead of merely establishing convergence.

4. Deeper Insight Compared with the Kernel Variant  
   â€˘ In the kernel variant a single HĂ¶lderâ€“type observation suffices.  
   â€˘ Here one must recognise that square-summability gives only global control, then devise a two-step strategy (row bounds + size partition) to upgrade it to mixed-norm summability.  

5. Non-trivial Generalisation  
   â€˘ Setting r=2 and s=p recovers the kernel statement after collapsing the second index, but the new problem is strictly stronger: it covers every pair 2â‰¤r<s and any array of data, not just sequences, making it significantly more complex than both the original and the current kernel variant.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let 2 \leq  r < s < \infty  be fixed real numbers.  
For every pair (m,n)\in \mathbb{N}\times \mathbb{N} let u_{m,n}, v_{m,n}\in \mathbb{C} satisfy  

  \sum _{m=1}^{\infty }\sum _{n=1}^{\infty }|u_{m,n}|^{2}<\infty  and \sum _{m=1}^{\infty }\sum _{n=1}^{\infty }|v_{m,n}|^{2}<\infty .  

Put  

  S_{r,s}(u,v):=\sum _{m=1}^{\infty }\Bigl(\sum _{n=1}^{\infty }|u_{m,n}-v_{m,n}|^{r}\Bigr)^{s/r}.  

(a)  Prove that S_{r,s}(u,v) is finite.  

(b)  Show the quantitative estimate  

  S_{r,s}(u,v) \leq  2^{\,s/2}\Bigl( \sum _{m=1}^{\infty }\sum _{n=1}^{\infty }\bigl(|u_{m,n}|^{2}+|v_{m,n}|^{2}\bigr)\Bigr)^{s/2}.     (\star )

(c)  Prove that the constant 2^{s/2} in (\star ) is optimal: for every \varepsilon >0 there exist double-arrays (u_{m,n}), (v_{m,n}) that satisfy the square-summability hypotheses and for which  

  S_{r,s}(u,v) > (2^{s/2}-\varepsilon )\Bigl( \sum _{m,n}\bigl(|u_{m,n}|^{2}+|v_{m,n}|^{2}\bigr)\Bigr)^{s/2}.  

In particular, no smaller universal constant (depending only on r and s) can replace 2^{s/2} in (\star ).
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout set d_{m,n}:=u_{m,n}-v_{m,n}.  

Step 1.  A uniform \ell ^2-bound for the differences  
For every complex numbers u,v we have  

  |u|^{2}+|v|^{2}=\frac{1}{2}\,(|u+v|^{2}+|u-v|^{2}) \geq  \frac{1}{2}\,|u-v|^{2},  

hence  

  |d_{m,n}|^{2} \leq  2\bigl(|u_{m,n}|^{2}+|v_{m,n}|^{2}\bigr).                                              (1)

Because the right-hand side is absolutely summable,  

  D:=\sum _{m,n}|d_{m,n}|^{2} <\infty .                                                                            (2)

Step 2.  Row sums  
Define S_{m}:=\sum _{n=1}^{\infty }|d_{m,n}|^{2}.  
Then \sum _{m=1}^{\infty }S_{m}=D.                                                                                  (3)

Step 3.  Comparing row-wise \ell ^{r} and \ell ^{2} norms  
Because r \geq  2, the \ell ^{p} norms decrease with p, thus  

  (\sum _{n}|d_{m,n}|^{r})^{1/r} \leq  (\sum _{n}|d_{m,n}|^{2})^{1/2}=S_{m}^{1/2}.                                (4)

Raise (4) to the power s to obtain  

  (\sum _{n}|d_{m,n}|^{r})^{s/r} \leq  S_{m}^{s/2}.                                                             (5)

Step 4.  Splitting the index set  
Partition \mathbb{N} into  

  A := {m : S_{m} \geq  1},  B := {m : 0 \leq  S_{m}<1}.  

Because each m\in A contributes at least 1 to the sum in (3), A is finite. Therefore  

  \sum _{m\in A}S_{m}^{s/2}<\infty                                                                                     (6)

(as s/2>1).  

For m\in B we have S_{m}<1, hence S_{m}^{s/2} \leq  S_{m}.  Using (3) we get  

  \sum _{m\in B}S_{m}^{s/2} \leq  \sum _{m\in B}S_{m} \leq  D<\infty .                                                              (7)

Step 5.  Convergence of S_{r,s}(u,v)  
Combine (5), (6) and (7):  

  S_{r,s}(u,v)=\sum _{m}(\sum _{n}|d_{m,n}|^{r})^{s/r} \leq  \sum _{m}S_{m}^{s/2}<\infty .                                  (8)

This settles part (a).

Step 6.  Quantitative estimate  
Write T_{m}:=\sum _{n}\bigl(|u_{m,n}|^{2}+|v_{m,n}|^{2}\bigr).  
By (1) we have S_{m} \leq  2T_{m}.  Hence from (5)  

  (\sum _{n}|d_{m,n}|^{r})^{s/r} \leq  S_{m}^{s/2} \leq  (2T_{m})^{s/2}=2^{s/2}T_{m}^{s/2}.                        (9)

Summing over m and using the convexity inequality  
  \sum _{m}T_{m}^{s/2} \leq  (\sum _{m}T_{m})^{s/2} (s/2 > 1),  
we obtain  

  S_{r,s}(u,v) \leq  2^{s/2}\sum _{m}T_{m}^{s/2} \leq  2^{s/2}\Bigl(\sum _{m}T_{m}\Bigr)^{s/2}.                       (10)

But \sum _{m}T_{m}=\sum _{m,n}\bigl(|u_{m,n}|^{2}+|v_{m,n}|^{2}\bigr), so (10) is exactly (\star ).  
This completes part (b).

Step 7.  Optimality of the constant  
Fix \varepsilon >0 and choose N large enough so that (1+\varepsilon )^{1/s}<1+\varepsilon /2.  
Define arrays  

  u_{1,1}=1, v_{1,1}=-1, and u_{m,n}=v_{m,n}=0 for all (m,n)\neq (1,1).  

Then \sum _{m,n}(|u_{m,n}|^{2}+|v_{m,n}|^{2})=2, while  

  S_{r,s}(u,v)=|u_{1,1}-v_{1,1}|^{s}=2^{s}.  

Consequently  

  S_{r,s}(u,v)=2^{s}=2^{s/2}(2)^{s/2}  
       > (2^{s/2}-\varepsilon )(2)^{s/2} = (2^{s/2}-\varepsilon )\Bigl(\sum _{m,n}(|u_{m,n}|^{2}+|v_{m,n}|^{2})\Bigr)^{s/2}.  

Since \varepsilon >0 is arbitrary, the factor 2^{s/2} cannot be replaced by a smaller universal constant.  
This proves part (c) and finishes the solution.
